Hopefully, by this point, you see the importance and usefulness of being able to bend the reality of your counterpart. These are powerful skills. Use them for good and not evil. Remember, if you poison the well of your community, then eventually no one will want to deal with you. You'll continually have a steeper mountain to climb until you just can't climb it anymore. These tools aren't about getting someone to bend to your will. They are about guiding your counterpart's perspective so that it aligns with yours in order to improve outcomes. When you don't change their frame of mind, you get compromise, which leads to bad deals for both sides. Sometimes the best option is no deal. People compromise because it is the easy thing to do, and that becomes a downward spiral. I am challenging you to take the road less traveled, not because it's the right thing to do, but because it's the best way to build your long-term success. Studies show emotional intelligence is closely tied to success in the business world. Think Oprah's success, think Warren Buffett's success, Richard Branch and success. All three have been accused of having tremendous amounts of EQ. Get used to the discomfort and you'll be able to handle any situation with poise. Navigating the complex world of emotions is difficult. Remember, emotion plays a role in every decision. Although you might feel like you're making a logical choice, your emotions, what you care about, drives you to your final decision in the end. Understanding how emotions play a role in negotiations can help you reshape your counterpart's reality, or understand how they are attempting to amend yours. All humans want to feel like they're being treated with respect. That is why the word fair is the most powerful word in any negotiation. What is fair to one person may not seem fair to the other. The F-bomb has a tremendous capacity for altering the perception of reality. You will have counterparts that will try to use it in order to catch you off guard. Either way, they may use fairness against you is by implying that you don't understand the concept of fairness. Either way, don't get suckered into a concession. Take a deep breath and ask them to explain how you're mistreating them. Fairness can also be used in a very impactful way that fosters positivity and trust. Towards the beginning of the negotiation, simply say, I want you to feel like you're being treated fairly at all times. So please stop me if at any time you feel like I'm being unfair and will address it. You'll lower their guard and they'll feel respected. Another dynamic that bends reality is deadlines. The closer you are to hitting that deadline, the more prone you are to making decisions that are not in your best interest. The stress and anxiety caused by rushing to meet a specific date or time are caused by that perception of loss that you'll incur in the future. Keep in mind that deadlines are often arbitrary and can easily be moved. Once you master this concept and make time your ally, then you'll have power over the situation. It's often smart to reveal your timeline to your counterpart. You actually end up with better deals as a result because you're eliminating the asymmetry of information and your counterpart will get down to business quicker. Don't forget, negotiations are anything but a straightforward path. They are non-linear events. Get the idea out of your mind that X plus Y equals Z. That isn't how it works. There is also always leverage, whether hidden or in the open. Being able to recognize the hidden world of needs and desires that lies beneath your counterpart's exterior is essential. It allows you to make your deal resonate with them. In order to earn real influence, it isn't enough to simply get your counterpart to understand what they can gain. Prospect theory tells us that what is actually important to them is what they may lose due to inaction. It's your responsibility to bend their reality so they can see it on their own.
